rationale: AI-Scientist v2 uses literature search during ideation. This placeholder marks ideas as provisionally novel until a separate energy-domain novelty checker is added.

raw.Name: counterfactual_restoration_probes
raw.Title: Counterfactual Restoration Probes: Learning Which Repairs Unlock the Most Grid Recovery
raw.Short Hypothesis: A small number of repairs disproportionately determine post-outage recovery because they unlock network reconfiguration, DER islanding, and load transfer opportunities. Current restoration and crew-dispatch methods optimize a specific forecasted scenario, but they do not explicitly learn the structural 'unlocking value' of candidate repairs across many possible outage realizations. We hypothesize that counterfactual probing—systematically estimating how much each repair action expands the feasible restoration set under uncertainty—can produce dispatch priorities that are more robust, interpretable, and computationally efficient than repeatedly solving full stochastic restoration models. Distribution service restoration is the right setting because restoration value is highly combinatorial and topology-dependent, and simpler proxy metrics such as critical-load proximity or component centrality cannot capture switching, islanding, and operational constraints jointly.
raw.Related Work: Prior power-systems work has studied repair crew dispatch, service restoration, and intentional islanding using MILP, stochastic optimization, and cyber-physical coordination. For example, recent work on coordinated repair of cyber-physical distribution systems co-optimizes physical and cyber repairs, and interdependent power-gas restoration work uses sectionalization and microgrid restoration under uncertainty. More broadly, distribution restoration literature considers switching, black-start DERs, storage, and resilience metrics. However, this literature mostly treats repair actions as decision variables inside a monolithic optimization for one scenario tree or robust set. The proposed idea differs in framing repair prioritization itself as a learnable scientific object: the marginal system-wide restoration value of a repair under many counterfactual futures. This is not a trivial speedup of existing OPF/MILP approaches, nor generic ML-for-OPF. The novelty is a topology- and constraint-aware counterfactual value function over repairs, trained from simulated restoration rollouts and used to guide crew dispatch and receding-horizon restoration. The contribution is specific to power-system restoration because the labels are defined through feasible energized load, switching reachability, and microgrid operability under network constraints.
raw.Abstract: Service restoration after extreme events is increasingly shaped by distributed energy resources, network reconfiguration, and limited repair crews. Yet current restoration methods usually embed repair decisions inside large mixed-integer programs tied to a particular uncertainty model, providing limited insight into which repairs fundamentally unlock recovery. This paper proposes Counterfactual Restoration Probes (CRP), a learning framework that estimates the restoration value of candidate repairs by asking a simple question: if this component were repaired earlier, how much larger would the feasible restoration region become across plausible future outages, renewable injections, and crew delays? CRP generates training data from power-system restoration simulations that combine topology reconfiguration, DER islanding, storage dispatch, and crew routing on standard distribution feeders. For each damaged component, the method computes counterfactual uplift metrics such as additional critical load served, reduced unserved energy, and improved N-1-like recovery flexibility over a finite horizon. A graph-based predictor then maps feeder topology, damage state, DER availability, and operational context to a repair-unlocking score. This score is integrated into a receding-horizon restoration policy either as a dispatch heuristic or as a cut/priority signal for a mixed-integer optimizer. The central hypothesis is that learning repair actions with high structural unlocking value yields restoration plans that are more robust to uncertainty than scenario-specific optimization alone. If successful, the work would introduce a new, interpretable interface between optimization and machine learning for distribution resilience, enabling faster post-disaster decisions with clear operational meaning and modest computational requirements.
Experiments: 1. Benchmark setup: Use modified IEEE 33-bus, 123-bus, and a larger synthetic distribution feeder with switches, critical loads, DERs, and storage. Simulate storm damage scenarios with line, switch, communication, and transformer outages, plus uncertainty in PV, load, and crew travel time. Implement a standard restoration MILP with switching, radiality, voltage/current limits, intentional islanding, and crew routing as the optimization backbone.

2. Define counterfactual labels: For each state-action pair during restoration, compute the uplift from repairing component i next versus delaying it for k steps. Label metrics include incremental critical load restored, total energy not served reduction over horizon H, number of additional feasible switching actions unlocked, and microgrid survivability duration. Aggregate these into an unlocking score using either scalarization or Pareto ranking.

3. Learn restoration probes: Train a graph neural network or simpler topology-aware model to predict unlocking scores from feeder graph, damaged-component attributes, electrical distances, load criticality, DER locations, and current energized islands. Compare to simpler baselines: degree/betweenness centrality, shortest-path-to-critical-load, myopic load pickup, and repeated deterministic MILP ranking.

4. Decision integration: Use predicted scores in two ways: (a) as a repair priority heuristic for crew dispatch with receding-horizon OPF feasibility checks, and (b) as warm-start ordering or branching priorities for the full MILP. Measure solution time, optimality gap, restoration quality, and robustness under out-of-distribution scenarios.

5. Uncertainty robustness study: Train on one storm distribution and test on shifted damage patterns, DER outages, communication failures, and travel-time uncertainty. Evaluate whether the learned probe outperforms scenario-specific optimization tuned to nominal distributions. Metrics: expected energy not served, weighted outage duration for critical customers, percentage load restored at 1/2/4/8 hours, and computation time.

6. Interpretability analysis: Quantify whether high-scoring repairs correspond to human-understandable structural roles, such as tie-line enabling, substation reconnection, or island merging. Use counterfactual case studies and ablations that remove topology, DER, or operational features to show what information is necessary.

7. Optional extension: Incorporate cyber-physical dependencies by allowing some switching or DER control actions only after communication-node repair. Test whether the probe still identifies cross-network bottlenecks better than purely physical heuristics.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Counterfactual label generation may be computationally expensive because it requires many restoration rollouts; mitigation is to use small horizons, sampled candidate actions, and offline parallel generation.
If learned scores merely approximate existing centrality heuristics, novelty and impact weaken; strong baselines and stress tests on DER/islanding-rich feeders are essential.
Performance may depend on simulator fidelity and assumptions about operator actions, damage distributions, and crew behavior.
Generalization across feeders with very different topology or protection practices may be limited; feeder-invariant features and cross-feeder evaluation are necessary.
Integration with full MILP solvers may improve runtime only modestly on small systems; the strongest value may appear on medium-scale stochastic settings.
The method assists prioritization but does not replace safety checks, protection coordination, or operator judgment in real deployments.
